topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped stone. My thumb hovered over the subject line, a cold knot tightening in my stomach as I clicked it open. The words swam before my eyes: "Regrettably, the surprise celebration for Sarah has been cancelled." A wave of nausea washed over me. I reread the message, my breath catching in my throat. This wasn't a prank. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I shot up from the sofa, pacing the length of my living room, my hands gripping the back of the armchair so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every sound in the apartment seemed amplified—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—and it all felt too loud, too sharp. I needed to call someone, anyone, but my mouth felt dry, refusing to form a coherent sentence.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly. The notification confirmed it: the surprise party was off. A strange, hollow sensation settled in my chest, like someone had just removed a vital organ. My hands started shaking uncontrollably as I clicked back to the original invitation, rereading the cheerful details of the planned evening. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me. I dropped the phone onto the wooden floor; it made a surprisingly loud clatter. I started rubbing my arms, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break, feeling a flush creep up my neck. I walked to the window, peering out at the street, trying to anchor myself in something solid, but the world outside felt suddenly too vast and indifferent.

[story 3]

When I saw the cancellation notice, the air seemed to evacuate the room. My mind went completely blank for a second, a sudden, alarming void. Then, a surge of intense agitation flooded my system. I started pacing back and forth, my footsteps quick and uneven on the hardwood floor. I kept glancing at my phone, refreshing the message as if the information would magically change. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. I ran a hand through my hair, messing it up in a futile attempt to steady the internal tremor. I felt a desperate need to move, to do *something* to counteract the sudden, unwelcome deflation of my expectations.
